verses.text: Satan (ou l’adversaire ) a incité David à faire un recensement de ses forces militaires (cf. **2S 24.1** ). Le Chroniqueur semble interpréter le récit de Samuel à la lumière de sa théologie de Satan en tant qu’adversaire de Dieu et de l’humanité. Comme Dieu permet à Satan d’agir dans le monde, Satan accomplit involontairement les desseins de Dieu (cf. **2Ch 18.3-34 ** ; **Jb 1.6–2.7 ** ; **Za 3.1-2 ** ; **Mt 4.1-11 ** ; **Jn 13.27 ** ; **1Co 5.5 ** ; **2Co 12.7 ** ; **1Tm 1.20 ** ; **Ap 20.3** , **7-9** ).

Satan (ou l’adversaire ) a incité David à faire un recensement de ses forces militaires (cf. **2S 24.1** ). Le Chroniqueur semble interpréter le récit de Samuel à la lumière de sa théologie de Satan en tant qu’adversaire de Dieu et de l’humanité. Comme Dieu permet à Satan d’agir dans le monde, Satan accomplit involontairement les desseins de Dieu (cf. **2Ch 18.3-34 ** ; **Jb 1.6–2.7 ** ; **Za 3.1-2 ** ; **Mt 4.1-11 ** ; **Jn 13.27 ** ; **1Co 5.5 ** ; **2Co 12.7 ** ; **1Tm 1.20 ** ; **Ap 20.3** , **7-9** ).\n\n

Comme Dieu permet à Satan d’agir dans le monde, Satan accomplit involontairement les desseins de Dieu (cf. **2Ch 18.3-34 ** ; **Jb 1.6–2.7 ** ; **Za 3.1-2 ** ; **Mt 4.1-11 ** ; **Jn 13.27 ** ; **1Co 5.5 ** ; **2Co 12.7 ** ; **1Tm 1.20 ** ; **Ap 20.3** , **7-9** ).\n\n
La note d'étude du verset 2S 24.1 expose les raisons possibles du mécontentement de Dieu au sujet du recensement.

21:8 David said unto God, I have sinned greatly: David's confession follows immediately on the realization of his census-sin (21:6-7). The triple admission — sinned, iniquity, foolish — borrows penitential vocabulary from Ps 32 and 51. Real confession names the act for what it is and pleads for divine erasure (cp. Prov 28:13; 1 John 1:9).
Gad est mentionné plus tard comme ayant rassemblé dans une liste des « événements du règne du roi David » ( **29.29** ).

21:10 I offer thee three things: choose thee one of them: The prophet Gad delivers the unusual oracle of optioned discipline. The three — famine, defeat, plague — match the covenant curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28. David is given the dignity of selecting his discipline, though all three remain within the Lord's hand (21:13).
21:11 Choose thee, &c.—To the three evils these correspond in beautiful agreement: three years, three months, three days [BERTHEAU]. (See on 2 Sam 24:13).
21:12 Three days - the pestilence in the land - In 2 Sam 24:13 (note), seven years of famine are mentioned.

21:13 let me fall now into the hand of the Lord . . . let me not fall into the hand of man—Experience had taught him that human passion and vengeance had no bounds, whereas our wise and gracious Father in heaven knows the kind, and regulates the extent, of chastisement which every one needs.

21:14 So the Lord . . . sent an angel unto Jerusalem to destroy it—The infliction only of the pestilence is here noticed, without any account of its duration or its ravages, while a minute description is given of the visible appearance and menacing attitude of the destroying angel.

21:15 stood by the threshing-floor of Ornan the Jebusite—Ornan was probably his Hebrew or Jewish, Araunah his Jebusite or Canaanitish, name. Whether he was the old king of Jebus, as that title is given to him (2 Sam 24:23), or not, he had been converted to the worship of the true God, and was possessed both of property and influence.

Ces versets offrent une description plus détaillée de l’ange et de la réponse de David que le texte parallèle ( **2S 24.17** ). La vue de l’ange accomplissant sa mission a inspiré David à plaider en faveur du peuple.

Ces versets offrent une description plus détaillée de l’ange et de la réponse de David que le texte parallèle ( **2S 24.17** ). La vue de l’ange accomplissant sa mission a inspiré David à plaider en faveur du peuple.

21:18 HE BUILDS AN ALTAR. (1 Chr 21:18-30) the angel of the Lord commanded Gad to say—The order about the erection of an altar, as well as the indication of its site, is described (2 Sam 24:18) as brought directly by Gad. Here we are informed of the quarter whence the prophet got his commission. It is only in the later stages of Israel's history that we find angels employed in communicating the divine will to the prophets.

21:19 David went up at the saying of Gad: David obeys the prophet's instruction to build an altar at Ornan's threshing-floor (21:18). The obedience is immediate and unquestioning — a contrast to the autonomy of the census that began the chapter. Restored kings move at prophetic word (cp. 1 Sam 15:22).

21:20 Ornan was threshing wheat—If the census was entered upon in autumn, the beginning of the civil year, the nine and a half months it occupied would end at wheat harvest. The common way of threshing corn is by spreading it out on a high level area, and driving backwards and forwards upon it two oxen harnessed to a clumsy sledge with three rollers and some sharp spikes. The driver sits on his knees on the box, while another person is employed in drawing back the straw and separating it from the grain underneath.

21:21 As David came to Ornan, Ornan looked and saw David: The Jebusite owner of the threshing-floor greets the king with deep obeisance. The encounter mixes royal authority with priestly humility — David comes not to seize but to purchase, modeling the rightful transaction by which the future temple site enters Israelite possession.

21:22 Grant me the place of this threshingfloor: David refuses the gift of the land (21:23-24) and insists on full payment. Worship that costs nothing is unworthy of the Lord — a theological principle the Chronicler underscores as the site of Solomon's temple is being acquired (cp. 2 Sam 24:24; Rom 12:1).

21:23 I give thee . . . the threshing instruments for wood—that is, to burn the sacrifice of the oxen. Very little real import—the haste and the value of the present offered—can be understood in this country. The offering was made for instant use. Ornan, hereby hoping to terminate the pestilence without a moment's delay, "gave all," oxen, the large threshing machine, and the wheat.
21:24 For the full price - That is, six hundred shekels full weight of pure gold.

21:25 David gave . . . for the place six hundred shekels of gold—At first he bought only the cattle and the threshing instruments, for which he paid fifty shekels of silver (2 Sam 24:24); afterwards he purchased the whole property, Mount Moriah, on which the future temple stood. High in the center of the mountain platform rises a remarkable rock, now covered by the dome of "the Sakrah." It is irregular in its form, and measures about sixty feet in one direction and fifty feet in the other.

21:26–22:1 Le recensement et la peste menèrent à la consécration de l'aire de battage d'Aravna comme site du nouveau sanctuaire. Habituellement, les offrandes auraient été présentées à Gabaon, où se trouvait le Tabernacle (voir **1Ch 16.39** ; **21.29** ; **2Ch 1.3**). L'arrêt de la peste à l'aire de battage permit à David d'y présenter des offrandes, conformément à la parole qu'il reçut par Gad le voyant (**1Ch 21.18**). David désigna alors le nouvel emplacement pour le futur Temple du Seigneur Dieu.
